statusLine: Americas oil markets hold steady at $101/bbl WTI despite mounting Middle East shipping risks and tightening US crude inventories.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets showed remarkable stability in the week to May 13, with WTI essentially flat at $101.04/bbl, gaining just 0.01% week-on-week. However, this surface calm masks underlying tensions as commercial crude stocks fell sharply by 8.6 million barrels to 384.1 million barrels, signaling tightening supply conditions. US production held robust at 13.71 million bpd, maintaining America's position as the world's top producer, while gasoline inventories also declined by 4.1 million barrels ahead of the summer driving season.

The WTI-Brent spread widened to -$4.66, with Brent at $105.70/bbl, reflecting heightened geopolitical risk premiums in international markets that have not yet fully transmitted to Americas crude. Critical maritime advisories remain in effect across multiple Middle East chokepoints, including the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, and Strait of Hormuz, where Houthi and Iranian attacks continue targeting commercial vessels. While these disruptions primarily affect Europe-Asia trade routes, any escalation could ripple through global oil flows and impact Americas markets.

Retail fuel prices continue climbing, with gasoline up 4.7 cents to $4.628/gallon, adding pressure on US consumers as the summer driving season approaches. Diesel prices held nearly steady at $5.639/gallon, though both fuels remain elevated compared to historical norms. The combination of drawing crude inventories, persistent Middle East risks, and seasonal gasoline demand suggests Americas oil markets face upward price pressure despite current stability.

Across the broader Americas region, attention remains focused on Guyana's rapidly expanding Stabroek production and Brazil's pre-salt developments, both providing crucial supply diversification away from traditional Middle East imports. Canada's oil sands continue trading at discounts due to pipeline constraints, while Venezuela's production remains hampered by sanctions and infrastructure challenges.
keyPoints: WTI crude held steady at $101.04/bbl in week to May 13, essentially flat (+0.01%) despite global tensions
US commercial crude stocks fell sharply by 8.6 million barrels, signaling tightening supply conditions
Critical maritime advisories active across Red Sea, Persian Gulf chokepoints due to Houthi/Iranian attacks
US gasoline prices rose 4.7 cents to $4.628/gallon as summer driving season approaches
WTI-Brent spread at -$4.66 shows international markets pricing higher geopolitical risk premium
US production robust at 13.71 million bpd maintaining global leadership position
